subject: Science

unit.unitOverview: Assalaamu alaykum, young scientist! In this unit you are going to explore one of the most useful and most exciting things in the whole world - electricity. Electricity is a kind of energy that helps us in ways we sometimes forget to notice. When you switch on a light in the evening, when your family listens to the radio, when a phone is charging in the corner, or when a fan pushes cool air across the room on a hot Mogadishu afternoon, electricity is quietly doing its work.
learningPath: Preview the goals and core ideas
Learn methods and study worked examples
Complete guided practice, experiments and games
Apply, explain and complete the Unit Challenge

provenance.transformation: Structured from the Cambridge Primary Science 0097 — Stage 2 workbook source documents for screen presentation.

outcomes: Name many devices that use electricity and say what each one is for.
Sort electrical devices by what they make: light, sound, heat, or movement.
Explain the difference between a device that uses batteries and one that plugs into the mains (the wall socket).
Describe how electricity flows around a complete loop, and what happens when the loop is broken.
Explain how a switch turns a circuit on and off.
Name two safety habits that Amina followed with her new lamp.
Use the word wall. Keep the Key Terms table nearby and check any word you forget.

concepts.example: Sort electrical devices by what they make: light, sound, heat, or movement.

explorations.title: Light Your First Bulb
explorations.context: To build a complete circuit that makes a small bulb light up.
explorations.prompt: Before you start, write your guess: Do you think the bulb will light when you join the battery to it with two wires? Why?
explorations.answer: When did the bulb light up - when the loop was complete, or when it was broken? What happened when you took one wire away? Why? Explain, using the word loop, why the bulb needs both wires connected.
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a complete circuit that makes a small bulb light up.

explorations.title: Make Your Own Switch
explorations.context: To build a simple switch that turns your bulb on and off.
explorations.prompt: Write your guess: What do you think a switch does to the loop when you press it?
explorations.answer: When the paper clip touched both fasteners, was the loop complete or broken? What did the bulb do? When you swung the clip away, what happened to the loop and to the bulb? How is your paper clip switch like the light switch on a wall in your home?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To build a simple switch that turns your bulb on and off.

explorations.title: Conductor or Insulator?
explorations.context: To find out which materials let electricity flow (conductors) and which do not (insulators).
explorations.prompt: Write your guess: Which will let the bulb light - a metal spoon or a plastic pen? Why?
explorations.answer: Which materials made the bulb light? What are these materials called? Which materials kept the bulb dark? What are these materials called? What do all the conductors in your test have in common? Why do we cover wires in plastic instead of leaving the metal bare?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find out which materials let electricity flow (conductors) and which do not (insulators).

explorations.title: Electricity's Four Jobs - A Home Survey
explorations.context: To find electrical devices at home and sort them by the job they do: light, sound, heat, or movement.
explorations.prompt: Write your guess: Which job do you think you will find the most of in your home - light, sound, heat, or movement?
explorations.answer: Which job did you find the most of? Which did you find the least? How many of your devices used batteries? How many used the mains? How many used solar? Did you find any device that does two jobs at once? Which jobs?
explorations.hint: Predict first, then observe, then explain.
explorations.explanation: To find electrical devices at home and sort them by the job they do: light, sound, heat, or movement.

methods.title: Light Your First Bulb
methods.example: To build a complete circuit that makes a small bulb light up.
steps: Tape one end of the first wire to the flat (negative, -) end of the battery.
Tape one end of the second wire to the bump (positive, +) end of the battery.
Touch the free end of the first wire to one side of the bulb.
Touch the free end of the second wire to the other part of the bulb.
Watch closely. Does the bulb light up? You have made a complete loop!

methods.title: Make Your Own Switch
methods.example: To build a simple switch that turns your bulb on and off.
steps: Push the two paper fasteners through the card, a little apart from each other.
Hook the paper clip onto one fastener so it can swing across to touch the other fastener.
Connect one wire of your circuit to each fastener, so the switch becomes part of the loop.
Swing the paper clip so it touches both fasteners. Watch the bulb.
Swing the paper clip away so it touches only one fastener. Watch the bulb again.

methods.title: Conductor or Insulator?
methods.example: To find out which materials let electricity flow (conductors) and which do not (insulators).
steps: Set up your circuit but leave a small gap where the two loose wire ends are.
Touch both wire ends onto the metal spoon, bridging the gap. Watch the bulb.
Now try the same with the plastic pen, the wooden stick, the coin, the cloth, and the rubber band.
Each time, watch carefully: does the bulb light up or stay dark?
Record your results in the table below.

methods.title: Electricity's Four Jobs - A Home Survey
methods.example: To find electrical devices at home and sort them by the job they do: light, sound, heat, or movement.
steps: Walk slowly around your home with an adult. Do NOT touch sockets or plugs.
Each time you see a device that uses electricity, write its name in the table.
Decide the main job it does: light, sound, heat, or movement. Some do more than one!
Write whether it uses a battery, the mains (wall socket), or a solar panel.
Try to find at least eight devices.

workedExamples.title: Counting With Batteries
workedExamples.prompt: Scientists sometimes count and measure. Let us do some gentle counting together with batteries. Remember, a single AA battery gives 1.5 volts. A 'volt' is just a way to measure how hard the battery pushes the electricity. The bigger the number of volts, the…
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 1. Idris has one AA battery. How many volts does it give? We know one AA battery gives 1.5 volts. So the answer is 1.5 volts. That was an easy one, but every good scientist starts with the simple case first. Worked Example 2. Now Idris joins two AA batteries end to end, the bump of one touching the flat of the next. When we join batteries this way, their pushes add together. So we work it out step by step: one battery gives 1.5 volts, and a second battery adds another 1.5 volts. We add them: 1.5 +…

workedExamples.title: 1. Idris has one AA battery. How many volts does it give? We know one AA battery gives 1.5 volts. So the answer is 1.5 volts. That was an easy one, but every good scientist starts with the simple case first.
workedExamples.prompt: Worked Example 2. Now Idris joins two AA batteries end to end, the bump of one touching the flat of the next. When we join batteries this way, their pushes add together. So we work it out step by step: one battery gives 1.5 volts, and a second battery adds…
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 3. Halima wants to know how many volts three AA batteries give when joined end to end. We add one more 1.5 to our answer above: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 4.5 volts. Notice the pattern - each battery we add gives another 1.5 volts. Counting in steps of 1.5 is good maths practice and good science at the same time. Worked Example 4. Omar has a torch that needs 3 volts to work. How many AA batteries does he need? Since one battery gives 1.5 volts, and 1.5 + 1.5 = 3, he needs two AA batteries. Working backwards…

workedExamples.title: 2. Now Idris joins two AA batteries end to end, the bump of one touching the flat of the next. When we join batteries this way, their pushes add together. So we work it out step by step: one battery gives 1.5 volts, and a second battery adds another 1.5 volts. We add them: 1.5 + 1.5 = 3 volts. Two batteries together push harder than one, which is why the bulb often shines brighter with two batteries.
workedExamples.prompt: Worked Example 3. Halima wants to know how many volts three AA batteries give when joined end to end. We add one more 1.5 to our answer above: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 4.5 volts. Notice the pattern - each battery we add gives another 1.5 volts. Counting in steps of…
workedExamples.solution: Worked Example 4. Omar has a torch that needs 3 volts to work. How many AA batteries does he need? Since one battery gives 1.5 volts, and 1.5 + 1.5 = 3, he needs two AA batteries. Working backwards like this - starting from the answer we want and finding the batteries we need - is exactly how grown-up engineers plan real devices. 📌 Remember! One AA battery = 1.5 volts. Two joined end to end = 3 volts. Three = 4.5 volts. More batteries push harder, so the bulb can shine brighter. But we always stay gentle and safe…

workedExamples.title: 3. Halima wants to know how many volts three AA batteries give when joined end to end. We add one more 1.5 to our answer above: 1.5 + 1.5 + 1.5 = 4.5 volts. Notice the pattern - each battery we add gives another 1.5 volts. Counting in steps of 1.5 is good maths practice and good science at the same time.
workedExamples.prompt: Worked Example 4. Omar has a torch that needs 3 volts to work. How many AA batteries does he need? Since one battery gives 1.5 volts, and 1.5 + 1.5 = 3, he needs two AA batteries. Working backwards like this - starting from the answer we want and finding the…

For…

workedExamples.title: 4. Omar has a torch that needs 3 volts to work. How many AA batteries does he need? Since one battery gives 1.5 volts, and 1.5 + 1.5 = 3, he needs two AA batteries. Working backwards like this - starting from the answer we want and finding the batteries we need - is exactly how grown-up engineers plan real devices.
workedExamples.prompt: 📌 Remember! One AA battery = 1.5 volts. Two joined end to end = 3 volts. Three = 4.5 volts. More batteries push harder, so the bulb can shine brighter. But we always stay gentle and safe with small batteries.
workedExamples.solution: A Science Story: Amina's Solar Lamp Read this short story carefully. Then answer the questions underneath. This will help you connect what you have learned to real life. Amina lived with her family in a small home outside Hargeisa. In the evenings, when the sun set behind the hills, the house grew dark. For a long time, the family used an old oil lamp, but its light was weak and it made the air smell of smoke. Amina found it hard to read her books by its small flame. One day, Amina's uncle brought home a solar…

workedExamples.title: Guided example 6
workedExamples.prompt: Electricity is a kind of: (a) food (b) energy (c) water (d) rock
workedExamples.solution: b) energy. Electricity is a form of energy that flows through wires.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 7
workedExamples.prompt: A complete loop that electricity flows around is called a: (a) circle (b) circuit (c) socket (d) switch
workedExamples.solution: b) circuit. A circuit is the complete loop electricity travels around.

workedExamples.title: Guided example 8
workedExamples.prompt: Which of these makes electricity you can carry around? (a) a battery (b) a wall socket (c) a window (d) a book
workedExamples.solution: a) a battery. A battery is a small store of electricity you can carry.

practice.prompt: Electricity is a kind of: (a) food (b) energy (c) water (d) rock
practice.answer: b) energy. Electricity is a form of energy that flows through wires.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A complete loop that electricity flows around is called a: (a) circle (b) circuit (c) socket (d) switch
practice.answer: b) circuit. A circuit is the complete loop electricity travels around.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which of these makes electricity you can carry around? (a) a battery (b) a wall socket (c) a window (d) a book
practice.answer: a) a battery. A battery is a small store of electricity you can carry.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A device that turns electricity into light is a: (a) fan (b) bulb (c) radio (d) kettle
practice.answer: b) bulb. A bulb changes electricity into light energy.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A radio turns electricity into: (a) light (b) heat (c) sound (d) movement
practice.answer: c) sound. A radio changes electricity into sound.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A fan turns electricity into: (a) sound (b) movement (c) light (d) heat
practice.answer: b) movement. A fan spins its blades, so it makes movement.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material is a good conductor? (a) plastic (b) wood (c) metal (d) rubber
practice.answer: c) metal. Metals let electricity flow easily, so they are conductors.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which material is a good insulator? (a) copper (b) plastic (c) a coin (d) a metal spoon
practice.answer: b) plastic. Plastic stops electricity flowing, so it is an insulator.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A solar panel turns ______ into electricity. (a) water (b) sunlight (c) sound (d) wind
practice.answer: b) sunlight. A solar panel turns sunlight into electricity.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: Which is SAFE to use for a science experiment? (a) a wall socket (b) a 1.5V AA battery (c) a damaged wire (d) a wet plug
practice.answer: b) a 1.5V AA battery. Small batteries are gentle and safe; the others are dangerous.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: What should you do if you see a wire with the metal showing? (a) touch it (b) fix it yourself (c) tell an adult (d) pour water on it
practice.answer: c) tell an adult. Never touch or fix a damaged wire yourself.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

practice.prompt: A switch that is turned OFF makes the loop: (a) complete (b) broken (c) wet (d) hot
practice.answer: b) broken. An off switch opens a gap, breaking the loop.
practice.hint: Use the unit's key words and explain your thinking.

activities.title: Light Your First Bulb
activities.materials: One AA battery (1.5V); A small bulb (a torch bulb) or a small LED from an old toy; Two short pieces of wire (for example, from an old charger cable, with the ends bared by an adult); A little sticky tape; An adult helper
steps: Tape one end of the first wire to the flat (negative, -) end of the battery.
Tape one end of the second wire to the bump (positive, +) end of the battery.
Touch the free end of the first wire to one side of the bulb.
Touch the free end of the second wire to the other part of the bulb.
Watch closely. Does the bulb light up? You have made a complete loop!
Safety: Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Make Your Own Switch
activities.materials: Your working circuit from Experiment 1; A metal paper clip; Two metal paper fasteners (brass pins) or two drawing pins; A small piece of card or thick paper; An adult helper
steps: Push the two paper fasteners through the card, a little apart from each other.
Hook the paper clip onto one fastener so it can swing across to touch the other fastener.
Connect one wire of your circuit to each fastener, so the switch becomes part of the loop.
Swing the paper clip so it touches both fasteners. Watch the bulb.
Swing the paper clip away so it touches only one fastener. Watch the bulb again.

Safety: Pins and nails have sharp points — ask an adult to help, and store them safely afterwards. Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Conductor or Insulator?
activities.materials: Your circuit from Experiment 1, with a small gap left in the loop; A metal spoon; A plastic pen or ruler; A dry wooden stick; A coin; A piece of dry cloth; A rubber band; An adult helper
steps: Set up your circuit but leave a small gap where the two loose wire ends are.
Touch both wire ends onto the metal spoon, bridging the gap. Watch the bulb.
Now try the same with the plastic pen, the wooden stick, the coin, the cloth, and the rubber band.
Each time, watch carefully: does the bulb light up or stay dark?
Record your results in the table below.

Safety: Small objects must never go in your mouth, nose or ears. Keep them away from babies and toddlers. Use only a small 1.5 V battery. Never use plug sockets or mains electricity, and disconnect the battery when you finish.

activities.title: Electricity's Four Jobs - A Home Survey
activities.materials: A pencil and this recording sheet; Your eyes and ears (this is an observing experiment - do NOT touch any mains devices); An adult to walk with you
steps: Walk slowly around your home with an adult. Do NOT touch sockets or plugs.
Each time you see a device that uses electricity, write its name in the table.
Decide the main job it does: light, sound, heat, or movement. Some do more than one!
Write whether it uses a battery, the mains (wall socket), or a solar panel.
Try to find at least eight devices.

activities.title: Observe: Switches
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of switches around your home or outside.
Watch it closely for a few minutes and note three things you notice.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

activities.title: Sort: Using Electricity Safely
activities.materials: Notebook, pencil and safe things you can find at home
steps: Look for an example of using electricity safely around your home or outside.
Find examples at home and sort them into groups, then explain your rule.
Write or draw what you observed, using the unit's science words.
Explain your finding to a family member or your teacher.

terms: Electricity
A kind of energy that flows through wires and makes devices work.
Battery
A small, safe store of electricity you can carry around.
Mains electricity
The strong, dangerous electricity from wall sockets. Never for experiments.
Circuit
A complete loop that electricity flows around.
Wire
A metal thread (a conductor) covered in plastic (an insulator) that carries electricity.
Bulb
A part that turns electricity into light.
Switch
A part that opens or closes a circuit to turn it off or on.
Conductor
A material that lets electricity flow through it, like metal.
Insulator
A material that stops electricity flowing, like plastic, rubber, or wood.
Solar panel
A flat panel that turns sunlight into electricity.
Motor
A part that turns electricity into movement, making things spin.
Energy
What makes things happen - move, shine, get warm, or make sound.

vocabulary.meaning: A kind of energy that flows through wires and makes devices work.
vocabulary.example: In this unit you are going to explore one of the most useful and most exciting things in the whole world - electricity.

vocabulary.meaning: A small, safe store of electricity you can carry around.
vocabulary.example: You may have felt the warm air from a hair dryer or heard a battery-powered radio playing across the compound.

vocabulary.meaning: The strong, dangerous electricity from wall sockets. Never for experiments.
vocabulary.example: Mains electricity is the electricity that comes out of the sockets in the wall.

vocabulary.meaning: A complete loop that electricity flows around.
vocabulary.example: Build a simple circuit with a battery, wires, and a small bulb (using only safe 1.5V batteries).

vocabulary.meaning: A metal thread (a conductor) covered in plastic (an insulator) that carries electricity.
vocabulary.example: A person who understands electricity knows never to poke anything into a wall socket, knows to keep water away from electric things, and knows to tell an adult when a wire looks damaged.

vocabulary.meaning: A part that turns electricity into light.
vocabulary.example: Build a simple circuit with a battery, wires, and a small bulb (using only safe 1.5V batteries).

vocabulary.meaning: A part that opens or closes a circuit to turn it off or on.
vocabulary.example: When you switch on a light in the evening, when your family listens to the radio, when a phone is charging in the corner, or when a fan pushes cool air across the room on a hot Mogadishu afternoon, electricity is…

vocabulary.meaning: A material that lets electricity flow through it, like metal.
vocabulary.example: A material that lets electricity pass through easily is called a conductor.

vocabulary.meaning: A material that stops electricity flowing, like plastic, rubber, or wood.
vocabulary.example: A material that does not let electricity flow through it is called an insulator.

vocabulary.meaning: A flat panel that turns sunlight into electricity.
vocabulary.example: You may already have seen a solar panel on the roof of a house or a shop, shining in the strong East African sun.

vocabulary.meaning: A part that turns electricity into movement, making things spin.
vocabulary.example: Inside each of these is a small part called a motor that spins when electricity flows into it.

vocabulary.meaning: What makes things happen - move, shine, get warm, or make sound.
vocabulary.example: Electricity is a kind of energy that helps us in ways we sometimes forget to notice.

commonMistakes: Thinking electricity is used up in the bulb.
The bulb uses the energy; electricity keeps flowing round the loop.
Thinking a dark bulb must be broken.
Very often the loop is broken, not the bulb.
Thinking all batteries are dangerous like sockets.
Small batteries are gentle and safe to hold.
Thinking you need many parts to light a bulb.
One battery and two wires can light a small bulb.
Thinking it is fine to touch switches with wet hands.
Water carries electricity and makes it dangerous.

connections.text: Bulbs are artificial light sources - they make light from electricity.
The same sun that gives day and night also fills solar panels with electricity.
Materials (Unit 2-4): Conductors and insulators are all about the properties of materials.
Counting devices, sorting them into groups, and making simple charts of your results.
Using solar power and saving electricity is part of being a good caretaker (khalifah) of the world.
Look for solar panels in your town and count how many you can find in a week.

realProblems.prompt: Use what you know to solve these real problems from daily life.
realProblems.answer: Any two battery-powered items, for example a torch and a battery-powered radio. (A phone or a solar lamp are also good answers.)
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Any two battery-powered items, for example a torch and a battery-powered radio. (A phone or a solar lamp are also good answers.)

realProblems.prompt: The mains power goes off in the evening in Mogadishu. Name two battery-powered things your family could use for light and news.
realProblems.answer: He should check that the battery is not flat and that the loop is complete (no loose parts or gaps). The bulb itself is often not the problem.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should check that the battery is not flat and that the loop is complete (no loose parts or gaps). The bulb itself is often not the problem.

realProblems.prompt: Hassan's torch will not light. Give two things he should check before deciding the bulb is broken.
realProblems.answer: A solar panel on the lamp turns sunlight into electricity in the day and stores it in a battery, giving clean light at night without power lines.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: A solar panel on the lamp turns sunlight into electricity in the day and stores it in a battery, giving clean light at night without power lines.

realProblems.prompt: Khadija's family wants light in the evening but lives far from power lines. Explain how a solar lamp could help them.
realProblems.answer: He should dry his hands fully first, because water carries electricity and touching a switch with wet hands is dangerous.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: He should dry his hands fully first, because water carries electricity and touching a switch with wet hands is dangerous.

realProblems.prompt: Yusuf's hands are wet after making wudu. He wants to switch on a light. What should he do first, and why?
realProblems.answer: Any two sensible ideas, for example: turn off lights when not needed, unplug devices, use LED bulbs, or use solar power.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: Any two sensible ideas, for example: turn off lights when not needed, unplug devices, use LED bulbs, or use solar power.

realProblems.prompt: A family wants to use less electricity to save money and care for the earth. Suggest two simple things they could do.
realProblems.answer: She should not touch it and should tell an adult straight away, because a damaged wire is dangerous.
realProblems.hint: Apply the science ideas to the situation before answering.
realProblems.errorFeedback: She should not touch it and should tell an adult straight away, because a damaged wire is dangerous.

reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Electricity is a kind of energy that flows through wires and makes devices work.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Name the four main jobs electricity does for us.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: The four jobs are light, sound, heat, and movement.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is the difference between a battery and mains electricity?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A battery is a small store of electricity you can carry; mains electricity is strong power from wall sockets. Batteries are safe to hold; mains is dangerous.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: Why does a bulb go dark when you remove one wire from the circuit?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: Removing a wire breaks the loop, so electricity cannot flow and the bulb goes dark.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What does a switch do to a circuit?
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A switch opens or closes the loop to turn the circuit off or on.

reasoningPrompts.prompt: What is a conductor? Give one example.
reasoningPrompts.modelAnswer: A conductor lets electricity flow through it. Example: metal (copper, a coin, a spoon).

questions.question: Electricity is a kind of:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: energy. Electricity is a form of energy that flows through wires.

questions.question: A complete loop that electricity flows around is called a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: circuit. A circuit is the complete loop electricity travels around.

questions.question: Which of these makes electricity you can carry around?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a battery. A battery is a small store of electricity you can carry.

questions.question: A device that turns electricity into light is a:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: bulb. A bulb changes electricity into light energy.

questions.question: A radio turns electricity into:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sound. A radio changes electricity into sound.

questions.question: A fan turns electricity into:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: movement. A fan spins its blades, so it makes movement.

questions.question: Which material is a good conductor?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: metal. Metals let electricity flow easily, so they are conductors.

questions.question: Which material is a good insulator?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: plastic. Plastic stops electricity flowing, so it is an insulator.

questions.question: A solar panel turns ______ into electricity.
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: sunlight. A solar panel turns sunlight into electricity.

questions.question: Which is SAFE to use for a science experiment?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: a 1.5V AA battery. Small batteries are gentle and safe; the others are dangerous.

questions.question: What should you do if you see a wire with the metal showing?
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: tell an adult. Never touch or fix a damaged wire yourself.

questions.question: A switch that is turned OFF makes the loop:
questions.hint: Use the Unit 5 Science Words reference.
questions.explanation: broken. An off switch opens a gap, breaking the loop.

selfAssessment: I can name many devices that use electricity and say what each one is for.
I can sort electrical devices by what they make: light, sound, heat, or movement.
I can explain the difference between a device that uses batteries and one that plugs into the mains (the wall socket).
I can describe how electricity flows around a complete loop, and what happens when the loop is broken.
I can explain how a switch turns a circuit on and off.
I can name two safety habits that Amina followed with her new lamp.
I can use the word wall. Keep the Key Terms table nearby and check any word you forget.
